file_name: Microgenics Corporation - Collaborative Development and Commercialization Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: During the Term and for a period of [***] ([***]) years thereafter (or such longer period of time as required by Applicable Laws), each Party shall maintain records in sufficient detail and in good scientific manner appropriate for obtaining and maintaining Regulatory Approvals.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term and for a period of one (1) year thereafter (or such longer period of time as required by Applicable Laws), each Party shall maintain records in sufficient detail and in good scientific manner appropriate for obtaining and maintaining Regulatory Approvals.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text reduces the record-keeping period to one year, potentially conflicting with FDA requirements for retaining records related to regulatory approvals.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 58.195(a) states that all GLP records, including raw data and documentation, must be retained for at least two years following the date of marketing approval for the product. For nonclinical studies, records must be retained for five years following the date the study is completed. This contractual change shortens the record retention period to one year, which directly contradicts this regulation.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the record-keeping period to one year, while 21 CFR § 58.195(a) mandates a minimum retention period of two years following marketing approval and five years for nonclinical studies (as stated in the Law Explanation). Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 21 CFR 58.195, further solidifying the relevance of this regulation. The contract's one-year retention period directly contradicts the legally required retention periods, thus making the contractual term unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: All payments due Microgenics under this Agreement shall be made by Achaogen in United States dollars within [***] ([***]) days after receipt of the applicable invoice by ACH transfer to the credit and account of Microgenics at the following account: [***].
perturbation.changed_text: All payments due Microgenics under this Agreement shall be made by Achaogen in United States dollars within 120 days after receipt of the applicable invoice by ACH transfer to the credit and account of Microgenics at the following account: [***].
perturbation.explanation: Extending the payment term to 120 days might violate California Prompt Payment Laws if Microgenics is a small business.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Prompt Payment Act, Cal. Gov. Code § 927.4
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Gov. Code § 927.4 generally requires state agencies to pay undisputed invoices from small businesses within 30 days, unless there's a good faith dispute. If the invoice is not paid within this timeframe the agency must pay a penalty. Modifying the payment term to 120 days would violate this statute if Microgenics qualifies as a small business under California law.

GENERAL [100 - 7931.000] ( Title 1 enacted by Stats. 1943, Ch. 134. ) DIVISION 3.6. CLAIMS AND ACTIONS AGAINST PUBLIC ENTITIES AND PUBLIC EMPLOYEES [810 - 998.3] ( Division 3.6 added by Stats. 1963, Ch. 1681. ) PART 3. CLAIMS AGAINST PUBLIC ENTITIES [900 - 935.9] ( Part 3 added by Stats. 1963, Ch. 1715. ) CHAPTER 4.5. Prompt Payment of Claims [927 - 927.13] ( Chapter 4.5 added by Stats. 1998, Ch. 916, Sec. 5. ) 927.4. Except as otherwise provided in this chapter, to avoid late payment penalties, the maximum time from state agency receipt of an undisputed invoice to the date of payment is 45 calendar days. If payment is not issued within 45 calendar days from the state agency receipt of an undisputed invoice, late payment penalties shall be paid to the claimant in accordance with Sections 927.6 and 927.7. (Amended by Stats. 2009, 4th Ex. Sess., Ch. 23, Sec. 5. Effective July 28, 2009.)

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment term to 120 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that this could violate California Prompt Payment Laws, specifically Cal. Gov. Code § 927.4, if Microgenics is a small business. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from the California Government Code, states that 'the maximum time from state agency receipt of an undisputed invoice to the date of payment is 45 calendar days'. The modified text, therefore, contradicts this law by establishing a payment term significantly longer than the legally mandated timeframe, potentially resulting in late payment penalties. The fact that Microgenics *could* be a small business is sufficient to establish the potential contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: During the Term, and for a period of [***] ([***]) years thereafter, all Confidential Information disclosed to a Party hereto or its Affiliates (the "Receiving Party") by the other Party or its Affiliates (the "Disclosing Party") shall be deemed confidential and shall be treated as such by the Receiving Party (meaning that the Receiving Party shall take the same steps to protect such information as it does to protect its own confidential information, which in any event shall be no less than the reasonable protective measures for the industry) and shall only be used for the purposes of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term, and for a period of six (6) months thereafter, all Confidential Information disclosed to a Party hereto or its Affiliates (the "Receiving Party") by the other Party or its Affiliates (the "Disclosing Party") shall be deemed confidential and shall be treated as such by the Receiving Party (meaning that the Receiving Party shall take the same steps to protect such information as it does to protect its own confidential information, which in any event shall be no less than the reasonable protective measures for the industry) and shall only be used for the purposes of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to six months may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under California law, especially given the context of a development and commercialization agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), Cal. Civ. Code § 3426 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Shortening the confidentiality period to six months may not provide adequate protection for information that continues to meet the definition of a trade secret (e.g., formulas, designs, customer lists), thereby undermining the legal protections afforded by CUTSA. The limited confidentiality period would also run contrary to standard commercial practices for protecting sensitive information disclosed under a Collaborative Development and Commercialization Agreement.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period to six months, which is likely insufficient to protect trade secrets under California law, as the Law Explanation states. The original contract lacks a specific duration, using "[***] ([***]) years", implying a potentially longer period. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing California Civil Code 3426 (Uniform Trade Secrets Act), confirms the existence of legal protections for trade secrets. The Perturbation and Law Explanations suggest this change undermines the legal protection afforded to trade secrets under CUTSA, especially when the trade secret maintains its status and competitive edge beyond six months. The shortened period creates a contradiction because it fails to adequately protect trade secrets for their legally recognized duration, rendering the modified clause unenforceable.
